title: The hand-painted house numbers
summary: Mrs Hue paints house numbers by hand. They are never the same. She says that is the point — a stencil treats every door the same, but she does not.
body:
On the small streets of our town, the house numbers are painted by hand. Some are big, some are small. Some have a circle. Some are blue, some are red.

The woman who paints them is Mrs Hue. She is fifty. She started fifteen years ago. Before that, she painted shop signs. Then shops bought computers and printers. They did not need her.

Mrs Hue thought she had no work. Then a neighbour came. The numbers on his door had washed away in a storm. He asked her to paint new ones.

She painted them. The neighbour smiled. He told another neighbour. The other neighbour came too.

Now Mrs Hue has more work than before. She walks from one street to another with a small box of paint. She paints two or three houses a day.

I asked her, 'Why do you not use a stencil? It would be faster.'

She said, 'Each house is different. The wood is different. The owner is different. A stencil treats every door the same. I do not.'

Some people say her numbers are not perfect. She says, 'They do not need to be perfect. They need to be ours.'

When I see a hand-painted number, I know which town I am in.

annotations.meaningVi: đối xử mỗi cánh cửa như nhau (không phân biệt)
nhiều việc hơn trước (cấu trúc so sánh hơn)
từ phố này sang phố khác (di chuyển liên tục)
hình tròn (ở đây: hình trang trí xung quanh số nhà)

grammarNotes.title: Comparatives: 'more X than…' / 'X-er than…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để so sánh, dùng 'more + tính từ dài + than' hoặc 'tính từ ngắn + -er + than'. Trong bài: 'Mrs Hue has more work than before.' (Bà Huệ có nhiều việc hơn trước.) / 'It would be faster.' (Nó sẽ nhanh hơn.) Cũng có dạng 'as X as' khi muốn nói 'cũng… như'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To compare, use 'more + long adjective + than' or 'short adjective + -er + than'. From the story: 'Mrs Hue has more work than before.' / 'It would be faster.' You can also use 'as X as' for equal comparison.

blanks.explanation: House numbers are painted by hand — each one made personally.
'Some have a circle.' — around the number.
Before house numbers, she painted shop signs.
The neighbour's numbers had washed away in a storm.
She painted them — and the work spread by word of mouth.
A small box of paint — her one tool.
A stencil — a cut-out shape — would make every number look the same.
The wood (of each door) is different — so each painting is different.
Some people say her numbers are not perfect — and she agrees that perfection is not the goal.
'They need to be ours' — recognisable as belonging to this town.
When the writer sees a hand-painted number, she knows which town she is in.

translationVi:
Trên những con phố nhỏ ở thị trấn chúng tôi, số nhà được vẽ bằng tay. Số to, số nhỏ. Có số được khoanh tròn. Có số xanh, có số đỏ.

Người vẽ các số đó là bà Huệ. Bà năm mươi tuổi. Bà bắt đầu công việc này mười lăm năm trước. Trước đó, bà vẽ biển hiệu cửa hàng. Rồi các cửa hàng mua máy tính và máy in. Họ không cần bà nữa.

Bà Huệ tưởng mình không còn việc. Rồi một người hàng xóm đến. Số nhà trên cửa của ông ấy đã bị bão cuốn trôi. Ông nhờ bà vẽ lại.

Bà vẽ. Người hàng xóm cười. Ông kể với một người hàng xóm khác. Người đó cũng tới.

Giờ bà Huệ có nhiều việc hơn trước. Bà đi từ phố này sang phố khác với một hộp sơn nhỏ. Bà vẽ cho hai ba nhà một ngày.

Tôi hỏi bà: 'Sao bà không dùng khuôn? Sẽ nhanh hơn.'

Bà nói: 'Mỗi nhà mỗi khác. Gỗ mỗi nhà khác. Chủ nhà mỗi nhà khác. Cái khuôn coi mọi cánh cửa như nhau. Tôi thì không.'

Có người nói các con số bà vẽ không hoàn hảo. Bà nói: 'Chúng không cần hoàn hảo. Chúng chỉ cần là của chúng ta.'

Khi tôi thấy một số nhà vẽ tay, tôi biết mình đang ở thị trấn nào.

questions.prompt: How are the house numbers in the town made?
questions.explanation: 'The house numbers are painted by hand.'

questions.prompt: How old is Mrs Hue, and when did she start this work?
questions.explanation: 'She is fifty. She started fifteen years ago.'

questions.prompt: What did Mrs Hue paint before house numbers?
Why did she lose that work?
questions.explanation: 'Then shops bought computers and printers. They did not need her.'

options: A neighbour whose numbers had washed away in a storm asked her to paint new ones
questions.explanation: 'The numbers on his door had washed away in a storm. He asked her to paint new ones.'

questions.prompt: How does Mrs Hue's business work now?
options: She walks from street to street with a small box of paint, doing two or three houses a day
questions.explanation: 'She walks from one street to another with a small box of paint. She paints two or three houses a day.'

questions.prompt: Why doesn't Mrs Hue use a stencil?
options: Each house, door wood, and owner is different — a stencil would treat them the same
She does not know what one is
questions.explanation: 'A stencil treats every door the same. I do not.'

questions.prompt: What do some people say about her numbers?
questions.explanation: 'Some people say her numbers are not perfect.'

options: They do not need to be perfect — they need to be ours
She will use a stencil next time
questions.explanation: 'They do not need to be perfect. They need to be ours.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say at the end?
options: When she sees a hand-painted number, she knows which town she is in
questions.explanation: 'When I see a hand-painted number, I know which town I am in.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last line.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mrs Hue says her numbers do not need to be perfect — 'they need to be ours'. Think of one thing in your town or family that is not perfect but is clearly recognisable as yours. What would be lost if a 'perfect' version replaced it?
